questions.stem: A consumer project imports a billing module with alias 'billing'. The module contains an agent named 'billing_lookup' and a tool named 'get_account_balance'. What are the mounted names in the consumer project?

options.text: billing__billing_lookup and billing__get_account_balance (alias + double underscore + original name)
billing_billing_lookup and billing_get_account_balance (alias + single underscore + original name)

questions.explanation: When importing a module with a given alias, all agent and tool names are automatically prefixed with the alias followed by a double underscore (__). So with alias 'billing', the agent 'billing_lookup' becomes 'billing__billing_lookup' and the tool 'get_account_balance' becomes 'billing__get_account_balance'. Double underscores are prohibited in aliases specifically to avoid ambiguity with this separator.

questions.stem: When the Runtime executes an agent and needs to resolve which LLM model to use, it follows a 5-level priority cascade. An agent definition specifies MODEL: 'anthropic/claude-sonnet-4', and there is also a project-level ModelConfig. Which model is used?

options.text: The project-level ModelConfig, because project settings always override agent definitions
The agent DSL model ('anthropic/claude-sonnet-4'), because Agent DSL (priority 1) is higher than Project DB config (priority 3)
The tenant-level workspace default, because it has the highest priority in the cascade

questions.explanation: The model resolution chain has 5 priority levels, stopping at the first match: (0) Deployment override, (1) Agent DSL -- model declared in the .agent.abl file, (2) Agent DB config -- per-agent override in settings UI, (3) Project DB config -- project-level tier-to-model mapping, (4) Tenant model -- workspace-level default. Since the Agent DSL (priority 1) is checked before Project DB config (priority 3), the model from the agent definition wins. Only a Deployment override (priority 0) would take precedence.

questions.stem: A consumer project can import a maximum of __________ module dependencies.
questions.explanation: The import limits state a maximum of 5 module dependencies per consumer project. Other constraints include: alias must be unique within a project, self-import is blocked, config overrides are validated (no secret key overrides, no template injection, max 50 keys, max 1024 bytes per value), and transitive dependencies are not supported (a module cannot import another module).

questions.stem: You are setting up an eval batch to test a booking agent. You configure an eval set with 3 personas, 4 scenarios, 2 evaluators, and 2 variants. How many total conversations and total evaluations will run?
questions.explanation: The eval set runs the Cartesian product of personas, scenarios, and variants for conversations: 3 personas x 4 scenarios x 2 variants = 24 conversations. Each conversation is scored by every evaluator: 24 conversations x 2 evaluators = 48 total evaluations. The documentation provides this exact formula with matching numbers as an example.

questions.stem: A module author wants to archive release 1.0.0 because version 2.0.0 is available. The archive attempt returns a 409 error. Which of the following is NOT a reason the platform would block the archive?

options.text: An environment pointer (dev, staging, or production) currently targets release 1.0.0
The release was published more than 90 days ago and has exceeded its retention period
A consumer project's dependency currently resolves to release 1.0.0

questions.explanation: The archive is guarded by a three-layer check, returning 409 if the release is referenced by: (1) environment pointers currently targeting it, (2) active deployment snapshots that have it frozen, or (3) consumer project dependencies that resolve to it. There is no time-based retention period for releases -- the immutability guarantee means releases can exist indefinitely. Published releases cannot be deleted while referenced by consumers.
